title: Te Aho Tapu o te Awa: The Sacred Thread of the River - Creating Art Inspired by Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural significance of Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a vital waterway in Ngāti Kahungunu rohe. They will learn about the pūrākau (traditional story) of Hinepūkohurangi, the mist maiden whose tears formed the stream, and how this connects to contemporary environmental challenges facing the awa.
learningObjectives: Create original artwork that visually interprets the pūrākau of Mangakōtukutuku Stream using at least two different media
Analyze and document current environmental conditions of the stream through photographic documentation and observational sketching
Incorporate traditional Māori patterns (kōwhaiwhai) and natural materials from the stream environment into artistic compositions
Present artwork with explanation of cultural connections and environmental observations to peers and local kaumātua

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa - Connecting to the Stream
activities.description: Visit Mangakōtukutuku Stream with local kaumātua who will share the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi and traditional knowledge about the awa. Students conduct observational sketching and water quality testing, documenting through photography and field notes.

activities.title: Te Whakairo o te Taiao - Carving Nature's Patterns
activities.description: Workshop on traditional kōwhaiwhai patterns and their meanings, focusing on water-related designs. Students collect natural materials from the stream banks (with permission) and create test compositions exploring pattern, texture, and form inspired by the awa.

activities.title: Hanga Toi - Art Making Process
activities.description: Students develop their final artwork using mixed media (water-based paints, natural pigments, collage with collected materials). They create pieces that respond to both the cultural narrative and their environmental observations, maintaining visual journals documenting their creative process.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga Toi - Art Exhibition and Korero
activities.description: Students curate a classroom exhibition of their work, writing artist statements that explain cultural connections and environmental insights. They present their work to peers, teachers, and invited community members, engaging in korero about their artistic choices and discoveries.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu for cultural guidance and storytelling
Water testing kits from Hawke's Bay Regional Council
'Te Māpara: Ngā Toi o Te Tairawhiti' resource book on Māori visual arts
Digital cameras and sketching materials for field documentation

tasks: Completed artwork demonstrating technical skill and cultural understanding
Visual journal documenting creative process and environmental observations
Artist statement explaining cultural connections and artistic choices

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context - Level 5: Investigate the purpose of objects and images from past and present cultures
Developing Practical Knowledge - Level 5: Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and principles
Developing Ideas - Level 5: Develop and refine ideas in selected art disciplines
Communicating and Interpreting - Level 5: Describe and respond to ways in which meanings are communicated
